coredata.dc:title: Thermal runaway management of Li ion battery using PCM: A parametric study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Srivastava, Gaurav
Nandan, Rakesh
Das, Mihir Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The paper reports an experimental and numerical study to manage the thermal runaway with in the safe operating temperature of Li-ion battery in terms of variation in its surface temperature subjected to different operating conditions. A parametric study has been conducted at different charging rates (1C, 2C, and 3C), and the cell's surface temperature is measured and examined. The study has incorporated three different Phase Change Materials (PCM) which are Eicosane, Paraffin wax, and Capric acid. The effect of varying thicknesses (5 mm, 10 mm) of PCM around the cell has been studied. The ambient temperature has been varied to examine its effect on the surface temperature of the cell. The results showed, using PCM around the battery cell reduced the cell's surface temperature by 21.5 % compared to the cell without PCM for the case of discharging at a 3C rate. Between Eicosane and Paraffin wax, the earlier performance is better than the latter owing to its higher latent heat. The cell with Eicosane reached a peak temperature of 313 K compared to 316 K for cell with RT 42. The surface temperature of the cell remained unaffected by the change in the thickness of the PCM layer surrounding the cell from 5 mm to 10 mm but the melting fraction decreased by 35 % and 25 % at a 3C discharge rate for Capric acid and Eicosane, respectively. The ambient condition of the cell is very crucial; it is found that at higher temperatures of the surrounding, the effect of the PCM is detrimental to the cell.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermal management
Li-ion battery
Phase change material
Numerical
Experimental
Cylindrical cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mesh independence test and Validation of the model
Effect of different C rates on the Li-ion cell without PCM.
Effect of different C rates on the Li-ion cell with PCM (Eicosane) in the experimental study
Effect of different C rates on the Li-ion cell with PCM (Eicosane and RT 42) in the numerical study
Effect of varying thickness of PCM layer around the cell for Eicosane and Capric acid in the numerical study
Effect of ambient temperature on the thermal performance of the cell with PCM (Eicosane and Capric acid) in the numerical study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Image of Li-ion cell - PCM assembly (a) isometric view and (b) top view.
Validation of the numerical model with the numerical work of Harrera et al. [29].
Single-cell discharging at various C rates.
Temperature variation in the li-ion cell with PCM (Eicosane) at 1C, 2C, and 3C.
Temperature variation in the li-ion cell with and without PCM at (a) 1C, (b) 2C, and (c) 3C.
Melt fraction of (a) Eicosane and (b) RT 42.
Temperature variation in the li-ion cell with and without PCM at (a) 1C and (b) 2C.
Melt fraction of (a) Eicosane and (b) RT 42.
Variation of (a) temperature and (b) melt fraction at discharge rate of 1C, 2C, and 3C with different thicknesses of Capric acid as PCM.
Variation of (a) temperature and (b) melt fraction at discharge rate of 1C, 2C, and 3C for different thicknesses of Eicosane as PCM.
Temperature Variation during discharging with PCM at different ambient temperatures at 3C.
Thermophysical properties of Eicosane, Capric acid, and Paraffin wax (RT - 42) [32,33].
Peak surface temperature of Li ion cell for different PCMs at varying discharge rates.

head.ce:title: Thermal runaway management of Li ion battery using PCM: A parametric study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Bhubaneswar, Argul 752050, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Bhubaneswar, Argul 752050, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The paper reports an experimental and numerical study to manage the thermal runaway with in the safe operating temperature of Li-ion battery in terms of variation in its surface temperature subjected to different operating conditions. A parametric study has been conducted at different charging rates (1C, 2C, and 3C), and the cell's surface temperature is measured and examined. The study has incorporated three different Phase Change Materials (PCM) which are Eicosane, Paraffin wax, and Capric acid. The effect of varying thicknesses (5 mm, 10 mm) of PCM around the cell has been studied. The ambient temperature has been varied to examine its effect on the surface temperature of the cell. The results showed, using PCM around the battery cell reduced the cell's surface temperature by 21.5 % compared to the cell without PCM for the case of discharging at a 3C rate. Between Eicosane and Paraffin wax, the earlier performance is better than the latter owing to its higher latent heat. The cell with Eicosane reached a peak temperature of 313 K compared to 316 K for cell with RT 42. The surface temperature of the cell remained unaffected by the change in the thickness of the PCM layer surrounding the cell from 5 mm to 10 mm but the melting fraction decreased by 35 % and 25 % at a 3C discharge rate for Capric acid and Eicosane, respectively. The ambient condition of the cell is very crucial; it is found that at higher temperatures of the surrounding, the effect of the PCM is detrimental to the cell.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Melt liquid fraction of the PCM

ce:section.ce:para: The population upsurge has been tremendous over the past few decades, which has increased the number of vehicles at an alarming rate [1]. The pollution caused by them cannot be ignored which amounts to 14 % of the total air pollution as reported by Global Greenhouse Gas Emissions Data for the year of 2014. Moreover, the depletion of fossil fuel reserves is a significant concern in the near future. Recently, electric vehicles have emerged as a potential alternative that fulfils the present need for transportation, reduces fuel consumption, and facilitates the sustainability of the environment [2]. Li-ion batteries are mostly preferred in electric vehicles than Nickel-Cadmium and Nickel Metal Hydride batteries because of their high specific energy density, high storage capacity, low density, low self-discharge, and higher number of working cycles [3–5]. However, the performance and reliability of the Li-ion batteries are dependent on the operating temperature. The desirable temperature range lies between 15 °C and 40 °C; if the temperature exceeds 60 °C, it will lead to battery failure [6].

Inside the lithium-ion battery are three major components: anode, cathode, and separator. The major problem associated with the lithium-ion battery is that its performance decreases when exposed to improper thermal management, mechanical deformation, or any electrical problems. These problems lead to thermal runaway [7], which causes an unstoppable chain reaction inside the lithium-ion battery, causing an explosion, fire, smoke, or internal short-circuit, leading to the electric vehicle's breakdown [8]. The main reason for the thermal runaway is an increase in the temperature inside the lithium-ion cell, which causes the separator to collapse, releasing stored energy and the gases produced because of the reactions. Cells exposed to high temperatures show reduced thermal performance and are prone to internal short-circuiting. To tackle this problem, we need a thermal management system that can efficiently dissipate the generated heat during the charging and discharging of lithium-ion batteries [9].

The thermal management of the battery cells is fundamentally of two types; active and passive cooling systems [10]. Active cooling systems [11,12] consume energy to create a forced convection environment, which is used to dissipate the heat from the desired location. We can use forced air-cooling, liquid cooling, and thermoelectric cooler as an active cooling system. In the passive system, external energy is not needed; the heat is dissipated entirely by conduction, convection, and radiation. Under the passive cooling system [13,14], various methods are available for heat dissipation like natural convection or air cooling, Phase Change Materials (PCM), metal foam, extended surfaces, vapor chambers, and heat pipes. PCM have the capability to store the generated heat inside the battery cells as latent heat of fusion [15–17]. If used in the correct proportion with an appropriate melting point, the PCM can maintain a safe operating temperature for the battery cells.

Liu et al. [18] studied the variation of temperature on the surface of lithium-ion batteries. They have used four cells connected in series at different discharging rates (40 A, 60 A, and 80 A) to study the temperature's transient variations on the surface of lithium-ion batteries and have concluded that variations on each cell of the battery pack are different. Sharma et al. [19] studied battery cells for the natural convection case and found that the temperature in battery cells increases much faster without a cooling system. Similarly, Elston et al. [20] studied the temperature distribution on the surface of lithium-ion batteries numerically under an air-cooling system. For the study, they have used the MSMD battery module of the Ansys Fluent with NTGK electrochemical model. They have used a high-capacity battery of 75 Ah with a nominal voltage of 3.7 V at the discharge C rate of 3.45 C. They found that the battery module generates very high heat due to insufficient air cooling. Instead, we can use liquid cooling for better thermal management [21]. Herrera et al. [22] studied the temperature distribution on the surface of lithium-ion batteries at lower C rates. They used a cell type of 18650 of capacity 4.39 Ah. They concluded that the NTGK model is suitable for studying the temperature distributions on the surface of battery cells. They also observed that radiation significantly affects cooling in the case of natural convection [23]. Menale et al. [24] experimentally studied the temperature distribution on the surface of lithium-ion batteries under air and Perfluorinated polyether cooling fluids. NMC and LFP cells have been used for the experiments at high discharge current. From the results, it is concluded that the position of the hotspot in cells is independent of the size and geometry of the cells. Perfluorinated polyether was found to be more effective than air for temperature reductions on the surface of the cell.

It can be inferred from the literature that air cooling (natural convection) alone is not sufficient to dissipate the entire accumulated heat because of the lower heat transfer coefficient and specific heat value. Hoshi et al. [25] studied the thermal performance of the PCM as the latent heat energy storage. Melting and solidifying the PCM cause absorption and release of the thermal energy, making it a potential thermal energy storage material. The study shows that PCM can be used for heat dissipation from the heat-generating source. PCM has the potential to act as thermal storage for heat generated in lithium-ion cells. Al-Hallaj and Selman [26] studied the temperature distribution on the surface of lithium-ion batteries with PCM. They studied eight lithium-ion cells of 100 Ah capacity numerically. For the study, Paraffin was selected as PCM having melting point of 56 °C, which was discharged at a 1C rate. Results showed that PCM smoothens the temperature fluctuations and reduces the temperature. Hamidreza Behi et al. [27] studied the battery module experimentally and numerically with natural convection, PCM, and PCM Graphite as cooling methods. They found that the average temperature of the cell module is minimum with PCM Graphite, and a reduction of 39.3 % is achieved compared to the case of cooling with natural convection. For 30 cell modules, the maximum temperature is reduced to 17.3 % with PCM graphite compared with natural convection. Duan et al. [28] experimentally studied the temperature distribution of lithium-ion batteries by replacing them with heated cylindrical casings. The melting point of the PCM used for the study was 50 °C. The results conclude that PCM helps make temperature distribution uniform and reduces the temperature on the surface. Weng et al. [29,30] studied the mitigating performance of composite phase change materials in a tubular module and traditional cuboidal module. They found that the factor of safety of the tubular module is higher than that of the cuboidal module which is 21.01 and 16.67, respectively. They also studied the honeycomb structure combined with forced convection and found that it reduced the cell temperature by 17.1 %.

It can be concluded that the thermal management of the Li-ion battery cell has been carried out using active and passive techniques. Active cooling delivers better performance, whereas passive cooling offers sustainability. Out of many passive cooling techniques, the PCM has the potential to serve as an alternative for the cooling of battery cells because of its high latent heat of fusion. Furthermore, the PCM's low thermal conductivity can be countered by adopting thermal conductive enhancers like extended surfaces, nanoparticles, heat pipes, etc. Many studies have reported the usefulness of the PCM as an energy storage medium and in maintaining a uniform temperature throughout the system. Most of the studies have focused on the effect of PCM on the surface temperature of the cell; however, not much emphasis has been given to the effect of the different melting points of PCM, PCM quantity, and ambient temperature. In this paper, an experimental and numerical study of a single-cell Li-ion battery has been presented. The thermal performance of the battery cell has been studied for various C rates (1C, 2C, and 3C). The effect of PCM (Eicosane, Paraffin wax, and Capric acid) on the temperature of the battery cell is studied. A parametric study with different PCM thicknesses and ambient temperature has also been analysed.

The schematic of the experimental setup designed and developed for the present study is shown in Fig. 1 . The surface temperature of the Li ion battery is tested under two conditions (i) Charging, and (ii) Discharging. The battery cell is charged in using a DC Power supply (Make: ULTRON ELECTRONICS, range: 0–230 V and 0–22 A). During charging the DC power is supplied to the battery, first at constant current and then at constant voltage. The battery is discharged by connecting an energy consuming device with it. A discharger (Make: ASTOR, range: 0–40 A) has been used discharging the battery cell at various C rates. The Li-ion battery cell is connected to the DC power supply for charging and the battery discharger for discharging. Thermocouples (Make: RS COMPONENTS, type: T type) of a diameter of 1.5 mm are attached to the surface of the battery to measure the surface temperature. The thermocouples are connected to a 16-channel Data Acquisition System (Make: NI Systems (India) Pvt Ltd.), which is further connected to a computer. A 3.7 V PSSS power Li-ion battery with a 3C discharge rating has been used for this study which is comparable to the discharging rate for the electric bikes. Specification of the Lithium-ion cell used for this study has been presented in Table 1 .

Since the safe operating temperature for the battery cells is between 15 °C and 40 °C, the selected PCM must have a melting point within this range. Eicosane as a PCM material has been used for the experiments procured from the TCI Chemicals, having more than 99 % purity. The thermos physical properties of the Eicosane have been represented in Table 2 .

The assembly of Li-ion battery cell and PCM (Eicosane) has been prepared, as shown in Fig. 2 . The glass tube (material: Borosilicate, upper-temperature limit: 160 °C) is used to thermally insulate the PCM from the ambient and observe the melting of PCM as well. The image of the experimental setup of the Lithium-ion cell and PCM assembly is shown in Fig. 3 .
The simulation work is done on the commercially available software ANSYS FLUENT. The NTGK method is used in the dual potential multi-scale multi-domain model in the ANSYS FLUENT.

Equations solved in the battery are as follows:

The detailed description of every term of the governing differential equation has been explained in Harrera et al. [31]. The heat produced because of the electrochemical processes is composed of two components: (i) the entropic component and (ii) the other is the overpotential heat.

Equations solved for PCM zone:

The following governing equations have been used to solve the PCM zone in the simulation. The energy equations used to solve the specific and latent heat are as follows:

Where,

denotes the density of phase change materials,

is thermal conductivity of phase change materials, H represents enthalpy of PCM, hs is sensible heat of PCM, β denotes the melt liquid fraction of the PCM, γ is the specific phase change enthalpy, Ts denotes the solidification point of PCM, Tl denotes the melting point of the PCM.
Boundary and Initial Conditions:

The ambient temperature is set to 306 K, and the heat transfer coefficient (h = 10 W/m2 K) is applied at the surface of the battery cell. The heat transfer coefficient for the simulation is selected by experimentation. The top and bottom surfaces of the battery cell are set as the insulated wall. The initial condition for the simulation is taken as, at t = 0 s, T = 306 K. The thermophysical properties of the PCM used for the simulation study are shown in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mesh independence test and Validation of the model
ce:section.ce:para: A Mesh independence study has been carried out for the single battery cell, shown in Table 3 , to check the effect of mesh size on the result and reduce the computational time. From the mesh independence test, it is evident that there is no change in the temperature up to two decimal places, even when the mesh elements are increased from 3993 to 68992. So, the mesh with 44,352 nodes and 39,146 elements has been chosen for all the simulations.

The numerical work has been validated with the numerical result of [22] for a single lithium-ion cell of 4.39Ah at a 1C current rate. The surface temperature of the battery cell of the present study has been compared with the [22], as shown in Fig. 4 . The results indicate good agreement with the published numerical work, as the difference in peak temperature is 0.4123 K for both studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different C rates on the Li-ion cell without PCM.
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of different C rates on the battery surface, the Li-ion cell has been charged and discharged at 1C, 2C, and 3C rates. Li-ions cell’s surface temperature has been measured with thermocouples. The simulation results are then compared with the experimental results.

A comparison between transient behaviour of the single-cell at different C rates has been plotted in Fig. 5 for experimental and numerical study. The peak temperatures reached by the cell are 325.5 K, 318.9 K, and 311.1 K for 3C, 2C, and 1C, respectively, in the experimental study. In comparison, the peak temperature reached by the cell in the numerical study is 322.5, 317.8, and 310 K at 3C, 2C, and 1C, respectively. From experimental and numerical results, it is evident that at higher C rates, the temperature on the surface of the battery cell rises, and the discharge time of the battery cell is reduced. At a higher C rate, the energy extracted out of the cell is higher, which leads to a faster chemical reaction inside the cell, thereby increasing the heat generation resulting in higher temperature compared to lower C rates.

A comparison between transient behaviour of the single-cell at different C rates for charging has been plotted in Fig. 6 for experimental and numerical study. In the experimental study, the cell's peak temperature during charging reached 321 K and 311 K, for 2C and 1C charging rates, respectively. In comparison, the peak temperature reached by the cell in the numerical study is 318.5 and 311 K for 2C and 1C charging rates, respectively.

During discharging of the Li-ion battery cell, the lithium ions travel from the anode to the cathode through the electrolyte whereas for charging it is reverse. As mentioned in section 3, the heat generation consists to two components broadly. The overpotential heat is always positive which is composed of ohmic losses in the cell, charge transfer overpotentials at the interface, and mass transfer limitations. Whereas the entropic heat can be positive or negative depending on the charging on discharging operation. During discharge the electrochemical process is exothermic and the entropic heat is negative but the charging operation is endothermic in nature and the value of the entropic heat is positive. In Fig. 6, for charging of the Li-ion cell at 2C, the deviation between experimental and simulation study is found to be eminent. The reason for such behaviour is thermal runaway. When battery cell reached a temperature higher than its safe operating temperature which is 313 K during charging, an instantaneous rise in temperature is observed. This leads to failure of the Li-ion cell. Hence experiments for charging above 2C has not been conducted. For the case of discharging the temperature reaches 325.2 K at 3C after 1000 s. The experimental and simulation study showed good agreement at 1C and 2C, but at 3C a minor deviation is observed. This minor deviation is due to measurement of temperature at a given point during experimental study whereas for simulation study the average temperature of the surface is considered. It is important to note that the temperature distribution in battery cell is not uniform. Therefore, there is a need to dissipate undesired heat generated to maintain a safe temperature of Li-ion cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different C rates on the Li-ion cell with PCM (Eicosane) in the experimental study
ce:section.ce:para: The transient behaviour of the cell surface temperature has been shown in Fig. 7 at various C rates for experimental study. The peak temperatures reached by the cell are 313 K, 312 K, and 309.5 K for 3C, 2C, and 1C, respectively, in the experimental study. When compared with the surface temperature of the cell without PCM, a temperature reduction of 12.2, 6, and 1 K is obtained at 3C, 2C, and 1C, respectively, in the experimental study. This suggests that at a lower C rate, the effect of PCM is not significant. The reason is that the PCM used in the cell assembly is Eicosane, which has a melting point of 308 K. Therefore, it is not entirely melted during the discharging process because of the lower peak temperature at a lower C rate. At a higher C rate, the effect of the PCM is evident. It can also be observed that a flat region of temperature is attained when the surface temperature approaches the melting point of Eicosane. At this point, the generated heat is absorbed by the Eicosane as latent heat of fusion, and it changes its phase from solid to liquid; since phase change is an isothermal process, a region of constant temperature is obtained.

By the use of PCM for thermal management, the surface temperature remained within safe limit even at the 3C discharge rate as seen in Fig. 7. Thereby successfully preventing the thermal runaway of the Li-ion cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different C rates on the Li-ion cell with PCM (Eicosane and RT 42) in the numerical study

ce:section.ce:para: The transient behaviour of the surface temperature of the cell for different PCM (Eicosane and RT 42) at varying C rates has been shown in Fig. 8 . At the 1C discharging rate, the peak temperature reached by the cell is 311.2, 309, and 312.3 K for cell without PCM, with Eicosane, and with RT 42. Initially the rate of rise surface temperature of the cell is higher for PCM assisted cooling compared to cooling natural by natural convection. This is because if the PCM is not melted, it can only dissipate heat through conduction and starts to act as an insulator thereby increasing surface temperature. It can be observed that for Eicosane, the peak temperature of the cell is lowest, whereas for RT 42, the peak temperature is highest. Even though PCM is used in both cases, the effects are entirely contrasting. This is because of the melting point of the PCMs; for a 1C rate, the peak temperature of the cell is lower; hence the Eicosane starts to melt, but the RT 42 is still in the solid phase. Therefore, the Eicosane absorbs heat as latent heat, whereas the RT 42 absorbs the heat as sensible heat. Since the thermal conductivity of the organic PCM is very low, the PCM (RT 42) acts as an insulator at a lower C rate and causes a rise in the temperature. But with the increase in the C rate, the RT 42 maintains a lower temperature than the cell without PCM because it starts melting. However, out of the two PCMs, the Eicosane maintains a lower temperature than the RT 42 owing to its higher latent heat value. The peak temperature reached by the cell is 318.5, 310.8, and 314.7 K for cell without PCM, with Eicosane, and with RT 42 for 2C, whereas for 3C, it is 324, 313.4, and 317.10 K, respectively.

When comparing the performance of the two PCMs for thermal runaway management, it can be seen that RT 42 is outperformed by the Eicosane as PCM. For 2C and 3C discharge rates the RT 42 is not recommended for the thermal runaway management. The melt fraction of the PCMs with varying time has been plotted in Fig. 9 . The maximum melt fraction obtained is for the 3C discharge rate, which is 48 % and 37.5 % for Eicosane and RT 42, respectively.

The transient behaviour of the surface temperature of the cell during charging for different PCM (Eicosane and RT 42) at varying C rates has been shown in Fig. 10 . At the 1C charging rate, the peak temperature reached by the cell is 311.5, 309, and 312.4 K for cell without PCM, with Eicosane, and with RT 42. It can be observed that for Eicosane, the peak temperature of the cell is lowest, whereas for RT 42, the peak temperature is highest. The effects are entirely similar to the discharging case. The peak temperature reached by the cell is 318.5, 311, and 315 K for cell without PCM, with Eicosane, and with RT 42 for 2C, respectively.

The melt fraction of the PCMs with varying time has been plotted in Fig. 11 . The maximum melt fraction obtained is for the 2C charging rate, which is 39 % and 30 % for Eicosane and RT 42, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of varying thickness of PCM layer around the cell for Eicosane and Capric acid in the numerical study
ce:section.ce:para: Studying the effect of the thickness of the PCM provided around the cell is crucial to optimizing the quantity of the PCM. Hence, the numerical study is performed for the PCM layer's varying thicknesses (5 mm and 10 mm) as shown in Fig. 12 and Fig. 13 . The results infer that there is no change in the transient thermal behaviour of the cell surface temperature. It can be concluded that changing the thickness of the PCM layer doesn't have any effect. In contrast, the melt fraction results showed variation with the change of thickness of the PCM layer. This outcome is certain since the mass of the PCM has been changed, whereas the amount of PCM melting is still the same. The melting fraction decreased by 35 % and 25 % at a 3C discharge rate for Capric acid and Eicosane, respectively when the thickness of the PCM is changed from 5 mm to 10 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ambient temperature on the thermal performance of the cell with PCM (Eicosane and Capric acid) in the numerical study
ce:section.ce:para: Depending on the ambient temperature, the PCM for cooling may vary depending on their melting point. Hence a comparative study has been carried out for PCMs at varying ambient temperatures (300, 306, 313 K), as shown in Fig. 14 and Table 4 . For the ambient temperature of 300 K, the effect of the PCMs is positive, meaning that a lower surface temperature is obtained for PCM assisted cooling compared to cooling by natural convection. But as we increase the ambient temperature to 306 K, it exceeds the melting point of the Capric acid, and hence it is in the melted state at the beginning of the discharging. Because of which it is subjected to sensible heating and no latent heat is utilized. So, it shows an adverse effect on the surface temperature of the battery and reaches a higher value compared to case of the cell without PCM. The same is observed for the Eicosane when the ambient is set to 313 K. The Eicosane and Capric acid thermal performance is lower than that of the cell without PCM. But when the thermal of performance of the Eicosane and the capric acid is compared, Eicosane performs better than that of Capric acid. The reason for this can be the thermal conductivity. The thermal conductivity of the Eicosane is higher than that of the Capric acid which is 0.28 W/mK and 0.153 W/mK, respectively. Eicosane having thermal conductivity dissipates more heat compared to the Capric acid thereby delivering better thermal performance. Hence, an ambient temperature higher than that of the melting point of PCM is not recommended for its detrimental effect on the surface temperature of the cell.

In this work, a 2.6Ah Lithium-ion 18,650 cell is studied experimentally and numerically for charging and discharging at different C rates. Temperature variation on the surface of the battery cell is studied for different PCM, PCM quantity, and ambient conditions. The obtained results have been analyzed, and the following conclusions have been drawn:
•
The numerical study's prediction of the temperature profile of the battery surface with time is found to be in agreement with the experimental results. However, the surface temperatures recorded by the experimental study is found to be higher than that of numerical study. This minor deviation can be due to following reasons: (i) for experimental study the temperature is measured at the selected points whereas for simulation study the average temperature of the surface is considered; (ii) the temperature distribution in battery cell is not uniform.
•
For the numerical study of a single Li-ion cell, discharging without PCM, the maximum temperature reached for 1C, 2C, and 3C is 310 K, 317 K, and 324 K, respectively. For charging without PCM, the maximum temperature reached for −1C and −2C is 311 K and 319 K, respectively.
•
In two PCMs that have been numerically studied, it is found that Eicosane is more effective as compared to the RT 42 owing to its higher latent heat value. Eicosane reduced the surface temperature of the cell to 313 K, whereas for RT 42 it is 316 K for discharging at a 3C rate.
•
In the experimental study, the peak temperatures reached by the cell are 325.2 K, 318 K, and 310.5 K for 3C, 2C, and 1C, respectively. For Eicosane used with battery cell, the peak temperatures reached are 313 K, 312 K, and 309.5 K for 3C, 2C, and 1C, respectively.
•
The surface temperature of the cell is found to remain unaffected by changing the thickness of the PCM layer surrounding the cell from 5 mm to 10 mm but the melting fraction decreased by 35 % and 25 % at a 3C discharge rate for Capric acid and Eicosane, respectively.
•
An ambient temperature higher than that of the melting point of PCM is not recommended and found to have a detrimental effect on the surface temperature of the cell.

The use of PCM based passive cooling maintained a safe operating temperature for the Li-ion battery cell owing to its high latent heat value. The use of PCM prevented the surface temperature to overheat thereby managing the thermal runaway of Li-ion cell. But, the use of PCM has an inherent challenge, which is its low thermal conductivity. Given that the thermal conductivity of the PCM is increased, its performance will be also be improved. Implementing thermal conductive enhancers like metal foam, nano particles, fins etc. with PCM can be further explored.
Gaurav Srivastava: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Rakesh Nandan: Writing – review & editing. Mihir Kumar Das: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
